participants.reasoning: The extracted criteria differ from the official record in several key ways, including the doctors’ English-language threshold, the women’s screening/fear requirements, and the specific exclusion reasons.
participants.extracted: The study involved two participant groups: general practitioners and women patients. General practitioners were eligible if they worked at least three sessions per week, practiced in a computerised practice, had been actively practising within the past 12 months, and had no more than 30% of patients who were non-English speaking. Women were eligible for screening if they had consulted a participating GP within the previous 12 months and were aged 16 to 50 years. For trial inclusion, women had to report being afraid of a partner or ex-partner in the last 12 months and indicate interest in being contacted; they were excluded if they no longer attended the GP, were false positives on the fear item, or were judged by the GP to have difficulties with informed consent, survey completion, or participation because of mental or physical health problems, cognitive impairment, intellectual disability, or poor English.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention closely, with the same training, counselling approach, target women, number of sessions, and usual-care control, differing only in added implementation detail.
interventions.extracted: The weave intervention is a multifaceted, practice-based program with professional, patient, and organisational components. The professional component, called weave Healthy Relationships Training, trains GPs to respond to intimate partner abuse when identified and to deliver a brief counselling intervention; it includes group discussion by teleconference, clinical audits, distance learning, evidence-based guidelines, two interactive practice visits, active listening exercises, attitudinal exercises, simulated patients, role play of readiness-for-change scenarios, survivors’ voices, and modelling of non-abusive behaviours, with additional practice, email, and telephone support as needed. The patient component consists of brief counselling delivered in the primary care setting by trained GPs to women who have been afraid of a partner or ex-partner in the last 12 months; women receive a letter inviting them to book appointments for relationship and emotional issues and may receive up to six 30-minute counselling sessions using motivational interviewing and/or problem-solving techniques to support validation, awareness, readiness for change, self-efficacy, and safety planning. The organisational component is minimal practice change, including informing administrative and clinical staff, placing posters, and setting up confidential reminder and recall procedures. Comparison group GPs and their patients receive usual care during the trial, and comparison GPs are later offered the training workshop after trial completion.
interventions.official:
Intervention (n = 162)
Doctors (n = 25): received the Healthy Relationships Training programme; 6‐hour online learning package and 2 x 1‐hour interactive practice visits for simulated patient role play with general practitioners (GPs). Also included the basic IPV education pack and continuous professional development (CPD) points. Programme delivered by academic GPs. outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include several measures not listed in the official outcomes (e.g., self-esteem, self-efficacy, social support, PTSD, smoking/alcohol use, and readiness for change), and the official text also emphasizes doctor education outcomes that are absent from the extraction.
outcomes.extracted: Primary outcomes were measured in women by postal questionnaire at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months following invitation to the intervention group for counselling. Primary outcomes included: quality of life, measured across physical, psychological, environmental, and social domains using the WHOQoL-Bref; mental health status, measured using the Mental Component Summary of the SF-12; and safety, measured by whether the woman had a safety plan (yes/no) and the number of safety behaviours enacted using the Safety-Promoting Behaviour Checklist. Secondary outcomes measured at the same follow-up points included readiness for change regarding the abuse, assessed with open-ended questions based on the Domestic Violence Survivor Assessment; comfort discussing abuse with the GP, measured on a 5-point Likert scale; GP inquiry about the safety of women and children, measured as yes/no; and anxiety and depression, measured using the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale. Additional variables collected in the 6- and 12-month surveys included sense of safety (visual analogue scale), open-ended safety behaviours, nature and frequency of abuse using the Composite Abuse Scale, health status (SF-12 PCS, smoking, alcohol use via AUDIT, and medications), post-traumatic stress disorder using the Short Screening Scale for DSM-IV PTSD, self-esteem using the Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale, self-efficacy using the Generalized Self-Efficacy Scale, and social support using the Oslo 3 Social Support scale.
outcomes.official:
Primary outcomes
Women's quality of life, measured with the WHO Quality of Life‐BREF
Safety planning and behaviour, measured with the Safety Promoting Behaviour Checklist

Secondary outcomes
